title: Managing a bokashi system in hot weather
subtitle: How heat accelerates fermentation, distinguishing white mould from problem mould, and summer bin rotation
excerpt: Bokashi fermentation speeds up significantly in summer heat. The bran works faster, the ferment completes in days rather than weeks, and the risk of unwanted mould or putrefaction increases if the bin is not managed correctly. This guide covers the seasonal adjustments to bran quantity and bin rotation that keep the system working through a UK summer.
sourceNotes: Effective Microorganisms Research Organisation (EMRO) technical guidance on bokashi. Garden Organic guidance on bokashi composting. Dr Teruo Higa, An Earth Saving Revolution (original bokashi research). Farm and Yard magazine guidance on seasonal bokashi management.

glossaryTerms.definition: An anaerobic fermentation process using effective microorganisms (EM) inoculated onto wheat bran. Food waste is layered with bran in an airtight container. The microorganisms produce lactic acid, acetic acid, and small amounts of ethanol, pickling the food waste rather than composting it.

A consortium of beneficial microorganisms including lactic acid bacteria, photosynthetic bacteria, and yeasts, developed by Dr Teruo Higa in the 1980s. Available as EM1 solution for making bokashi bran or as pre-inoculated bran.

content.text: At 18 to 22 degrees Celsius (typical UK kitchen),
takes 10 to 14 days to complete in a full bin. At 28 to 32 degrees Celsius during a July heatwave, the same bin can complete in 5 to 7 days. The
are more active at higher temperatures, but so are competing organisms. The accelerated fermentation means bins fill and complete faster, requiring faster rotation.

In summer, increase the bran layer per addition by 20 to 30 percent. The increased microbial activity means more bran inoculant is needed to maintain dominance over competing organisms. Standard rate: 1 tablespoon of bran per 500 g of food waste. Summer rate: 1.5 tablespoons per 500 g. Keep the bin lid tightly closed between additions; warm air speeds drying and spoilage at the surface.

White or grey-white mould on the surface of the contents: this is desirable. It is yeast or lactic acid bacteria forming a visible biofilm. It indicates active, healthy fermentation. The smell should be sweet-sour or vinegar-like.

Black or green mould: indicates putrefaction. The smell will be foul (not sour). Usually caused by too much air in the bin, insufficient bran, liquid in the bin that diluted the bran, or a bin not properly sealed. Empty the bin and compost or bin the contents; clean and restart.

A two-bin system is standard: one bin filling, one bin completing. In summer, the completing bin should be ready to empty in 5 to 8 days after sealing. Move it to a shaded spot outdoors (below 25 degrees Celsius if possible) while it completes; direct sun will overheat the contents and kill the microorganisms. The fermented material can go directly into a trench in the garden (bury at 15 cm minimum depth) or into a compost heap to mature for a further 4 to 6 weeks.

The bokashi liquid (drain from the bin tap) is more acidic in summer and more concentrated. Dilute to at least 1:100 before using as a plant drench. Undiluted bokashi liquid at pH 3.5 will damage plant roots. Empty the drain tap every 2 to 3 days in summer rather than weekly.

attrs.symptom: Bin smells putrid despite bran addition

content.text: The bin has been left in direct sun above 40 degrees Celsius, which kills the EM microorganisms. Or the contents contain liquid from cooked or watery foods that diluted the bran. Start a fresh bin and add an extra layer of bran at the base before adding any food waste.
